title: How EFT tapping works
subtitle: The eight points, the three rounds, and the short setup statement.
excerpt: A reference article for the tapping practices in the library. Covers what EFT is, where the eight points are, and how a session is structured. Read once; the practice scripts assume it.
sourceNotes: EFT was developed by Gary Craig in the mid-1990s, based on Roger Callahan's earlier Thought Field Therapy (1980s). The eight-point sequence and the three-round structure used here follow the standard EFT shorthand format documented in Craig's original training materials and reproduced in Rebecca J Page's MONEY (2025), SLEEP (2025), WEIGHT LOSS (2025), and MANIFESTING (2025) programs.

practice.whenToUse: Read once before starting any tapping practice in the library. Re-read if a script line feels unclear or if a long break has passed.

glossaryTerms.definition: Emotional Freedom Technique. A self-regulation practice combining spoken phrases with light tapping on eight acupressure points on the face and upper body.
The soft fleshy edge of the hand, on the side of the palm under the little finger. The starting point of an EFT round.
The opening line of an EFT round, said while tapping the karate chop point. Of the shape "Even though I [name the feeling], I deeply and completely accept myself."
One pass through the eight points (Eyebrow, Side of Eye, Under Eye, Under Nose, Chin, Collarbone, Under Arm, Top of Head), one short line per point.
A second pass through the same eight points, with each line reframed from the current state toward where the user is moving.

content.text: EFT (Emotional Freedom Technique) is a self-regulation practice that pairs light finger-tapping on eight specific points with short spoken statements. It was developed by Gary Craig in the mid-1990s, building on Roger Callahan's earlier Thought Field Therapy. The version used across this library follows Craig's standard shorthand.

Every tapping practice in the library has the same shape. A short set-up at the karate chop point, a round through the eight tapping points, and a reframe round through the same eight points. The reading below covers each part so the individual practice scripts can stay tight and get on with the work.
The eight points
The script moves through these eight points in order. Each line is tapped about five to seven times, lightly but firmly, with two or three fingers of either hand. Either side of the body works.
Eyebrow.
The inner edge of the brow, just above the bridge of the nose.
Side of eye.
On the bone at the outer corner of the eye.
Under eye.
On the bone directly below the pupil, on the upper cheek.
Under nose.
The hollow between the base of the nose and the upper lip.
Chin.
The crease between the lower lip and the chin.
Collarbone.
About 2 cm down from the inner end of the collarbone, where the rib meets the sternum.
Under arm.
About 10 cm below the armpit, on the side of the body, level with the nipple line.
Top of head.
The crown of the head, in line with the ears.
The karate chop point

The set-up of a tapping session uses a ninth point: the karate chop point. It's the soft fleshy edge of the hand, on the side of the palm under the little finger. The user taps it with two or three fingers of the other hand while reading the three set-up statements aloud (or under the breath, or in the mind — whichever the situation allows).
How a session runs
Every tapping practice in the library has the same three-part structure.
1. Karate chop.

Tap the karate chop point while reading the three set-up statements once each. The set-up statements are of the form "Even though I [name the feeling], I deeply and completely accept myself." They name what the round is about (the specific worry or pattern being worked with) and acknowledge it before the rounds start.
2. Tapping round.
Tap the eight points in order, one line per point, five to seven taps per point. Each line states the current feeling. Go through the round three times before moving on.
3. Reframe round.

Tap the same eight points in the same order again, this time reading the reframe line for each point. The reframe lines move the script from the current state toward where the user is going. Three passes through the reframe round.
Total time: five to ten minutes for a typical script. Twenty minutes for a longer one.
Using your own words

The scripts in the library are templates. If a line doesn't fit the situation, change it. Use the wording that names what's actually true. Rebecca rewrites the lines most days she taps; that's the intended use. The structure (eight points, three rounds, reframe round) is what holds; the words are adjustable.
Where this comes from

EFT was developed by Gary Craig in the mid-1990s as a simplified, accessible version of Roger Callahan's Thought Field Therapy. The eight-point shorthand has remained stable for nearly thirty years and is the format used by the EFT community internationally. The three-part script style in this library (set-up statements, tapping round, reframe round) was contributed by Rebecca Page, drawn from across her four tapping books published in 2025.
